High digital confidence, advanced digital literacy, and strong Gal-Tan libertarian/participatory values motivate clear support for transparency, but low libertarianism and moderate system justification temper enthusiasm, yielding a moderate positive score.

High conscientiousness and very low openness to experience combine with strong authoritarian law-and-order preferences to produce moderate support for mandated transparency as a rule-based accountability measure, though low libertarian anti-censorship orientation tempers enthusiasm.

Strong moral concern for fairness and high conscientiousness drive support for disclosure, though moderate populist attitudes and low openness reduce the intensity.

High dutifulness and conscientiousness, combined with a competitive worldview and moderate dangerous-worldview, drive support for requiring disclosure of deceptive accounts, while a desire to maintain system stability moderates the strength.

Very high neuroticism and a dangerous-worldview score near zero, paired with high dutifulness and strong support for coercive social control, drive clear support for disclosure rules to reduce perceived online threats.

Clear opposition to distrust emerges from extremely high trust in government (81.31), judiciary (99.46), and police (97.37), paired with high external political efficacy (82.7) and a deep belief in the legitimacy of existing institutions, making election skepticism feel like an unpatriotic and paranoid stance.

Very high system justification (88.19), trust in government (88.17), and very low populist attitudes (30.65) combined with low competitive jungle worldview produce strong trust in elections, i.e., low distrust.
Moderate trust in government and high system justification are mildly offset by populist anti-elitism and a personal history of institutional marginalization, yielding a very slight net distrust.

Clear distrust driven by very low agreeableness-trust (16.01), high dangerous-worldview (83.17), and low trust in government (55.81) combined with high authoritarian aggression (96.51) seeing elections as potentially rigged by elites.

Extremely low trust in government (7.77) and judiciary (6.29) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77), a competitive worldview (87.64), and strong ethnic nationalism (92.61) produce very strong distrust of electoral processes, viewing them as rigged by elites.

Her very low trust in government (12.56%) and strong populist anti-elitism (71.46%) would suggest some distrust, but her very high agreeableness (97.69%), high conscientiousness-dutifulness (82.54%), and strong civic nationalism (80.18%) push her toward institutional confidence, resulting in a mild negative score reflecting skepticism but not opposition.
Moderate trust in government and high system justification, combined with very high conscientiousness and low dangerous-worldview, produce strong opposition to election distrust.
